Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. TargetRLF1: A trichomoniasis test detects the bacteria that cause trichomonas vaginalis, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a wet mount. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for trich. In this case, your provider swabs/wipes your vagina and urethra to collect a sample. They send the cells to your lab to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of infection to detect infections in the anus or vice versa. Or they may use a swab to see how the bacteria are working. Because of the possibility of further complications, there is still a risk of further infection if the bacteria become infected. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another

TargetRLF1: Genital Herpes or Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV), also known as Herpes is a sexually transmitted infection that can cause painful blisters and open sores (lesions) in the genital area. Your need for testing depends on your personal risk for getting herpes.

TargetRLF1: The origins of the disease Syphilis can be traced back to the earliest records of the human race. The Romans, Jews and Arabs all have documents referring to syphilis and each society had their own description of symptoms and treatment. The disease has been referred to by various names in different societies. The Roman physician Galen in 130 AD described the disease as an "involuntary escape of semen". Hippocrates (460375 BC) called it "Strangury." The disease was also referred to as The Clap, referring either to the clapping feeling experienced by the infected person during urination. In those days, men were considered victims and women as the source. The organism that causes the disease was first discovered in 1879 in Albert Neisser, hence the organism being called Neisseria pallidum. It was first described in historical records in Ebers papyrus, written between 1553 and 1550 BC.

TargetRLF1: A gonorrhea test detects the bacteria that cause Gonorrhea, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for gonorrhoea. In this case, your provider swabs/wipes your eyes, urethra or anus to collect a sample and test it on it. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of bacteria test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use one of these testes to see how antigens are working. Your need for testing depends on your personal risk for getting Gonococcal. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear
